*并非所有加密狗都兼容SDR#和CUBICSDR。在购买之前，请检查加密狗是否与您打算使用的软件兼容！
适用于Windows和Mac OS的卫星解码软件我们使用的是WXtoImg的测试版(免费)。
MacOS 10.15 Catalina兼容性问题如果您运行的是MacOS 10.15 Catalina，您将无法下载、安装和使用WXtoImg。这是因为10.15 Catalina不再支持32位应用程序。如果你处在这个位置，你有几个选择：通过BootCamp将你的Mac分区为运行Windows10。然后在Windows分区上安装SDR软件、虚拟音频电缆和WXtoImg。当您想要使用前述软件来解码NOAA APT传输时，您将需要输入分区。
使用在Max OS Catalina上运行的替代卫星解码软件，如由Martin Bernardi开发的开源程序NOAA APT 1.2.0。NOAA APT 1.2.0比WXtoImg有更少的功能，例如，它没有假彩色图像和实时解码。NOAA APT 1.2.0不是像WXtoImg那样将音频直接从SDR软件传输到卫星解码软件，而是需要使用您的SDR软件录制卫星传输，然后上传WAV文件。该软件可以生成相当大、高对比度和详细的图像。请参阅活动帖子：如何在Mac上编译NOAA APT 1.2.0。
使用基于浏览器的APT解码器，比如Mac上的APT 3000。像NOAA APT 1.2.0一样，您需要上传预先录制的wav文件。然后，浏览器生成卫星图像。APT 3000产生的图像质量低于NOAA APT 1.2.0和WXtoImg，但它是一个简单的替代方案，不需要太多的努力和时间。
使用Parallels等虚拟机在Mac上运行Windows 10。这是一项付费服务，使您可以在Mac上运行Windows，而无需对硬盘进行分区。在发布台式机许可证时，费用为GB 69.99\。
一旦你组装好了硬件，需要一天到半天的时间，要有一点耐心，最好是找一个伙伴来做一些事情，比如在你调软件的时候拿着你的天线。
NOAA卫星是如何收集数据的？NOAA卫星传输的图像是由卫星的主要扫描仪器--高级甚高分辨率辐射计(AVHRR)产生的。该仪器被设计用来探测来自地球表面的五个通道的辐射能量，范围从可见光光谱到近红外和红外或热光谱。当卫星经过地球某一特定区域时，AVHRR传感器近乎实时地收集和传输数据。可以把这颗卫星看作是逐行扫描地球表面。在生成的图像中，每个像素大约为4×4千米。
它们如何传输数据？NOAA卫星内置了无线电天线，可以在137兆赫范围内的频率上传输AVHRR仪器收集的数据。为了尽量减少卫星之间的干扰，每颗NOAA卫星在137兆赫范围内使用不同的频率进行传输。
美国国家海洋和大气局的卫星是如何运行的？美国国家海洋和大气局卫星同步围绕太阳绕地球运行。太阳同步轨道是一种近乎极地的轨道，卫星每天在同一时间经过地球表面的某一给定点。美国国家海洋和大气局的卫星沿着低地球轨道(LEO)运行。LEO是以地球为中心的轨道，高度在2000公里以下。每颗卫星大约每100分钟绕地球一圈。
天线是用来做什么的？你的天线是个传感器。它捕捉电磁波并将其转换成电流，即电信号。所有天线都调谐到特定的频率范围，这意味着它们接收或发送这些频率的效果最好。大多数天线都是定向的。
射频电缆的用途是什么？射频电缆是一种绝缘的射频电缆。它包括由屏蔽导体包围的中心导体。电缆将电信号从一个点传输到另一个点(即从天线到加密狗)，而不会产生干扰。
加密狗是做什么的？加密狗接收来自同轴电缆的电信号，对其进行滤波，然后将其从模拟I/Q转换为数字I/Q，以便您的计算机可以读取。
你为什么需要软件？您需要软件来处理通过加密狗发送到计算机的数字信号。软件使这些信号看得见、听得见；其他软件将卫星发射的信号解码成可视图像。
需要哪些不同种类的软件？要解码来自卫星的传输，您需要三种软件：
软件定义无线电或SDR软件，用于解调天线捕获的无线电波并调谐到特定频率，就像传统无线电一样。SDR软件输出音频。
卫星解码软件(例如WXtoImg)，将SDR软件生成的音频解码成图像。
连接SDR软件和卫星解码软件的虚拟音频线软件…。
我如何练习使用SDR软件？练习使用SDR技术的最佳方式是将您的设备(天线、计算机、加密狗和电缆)带到附近的开放空间，插入所有设备，启动您的SDR软件，并享受探索无线电频谱的乐趣。您可以尝试更改模式(调制)、在业余无线电频段间滚动以及切换频率等操作。有关SDR软件的启动指南，请参阅下面的内容。
您如何将SDR软件连接到卫星解码软件？无论您使用哪种类型的计算机，也不管您喜欢哪种类型的SDR软件，您都需要将SDR软件的音频输出发送到卫星解码软件(例如WXtoImg)。您可以使用一系列免费软件充当SDR软件和卫星解码软件之间的虚拟电缆。@sophed使用SoundFloor(Mac版)，而@sashae更喜欢VB Cable(Windows10版)。
1.将天线连接到射频电缆。(如果您需要组装天线，请先进行此操作)。
2.将同轴电缆拧入加密狗的非USB侧(注意不要过度拧紧电缆接头，因为它们很容易损坏。不过是手指紧绷)。
这是您将用于解码气象卫星图像的基本设置。要使此设置正常运行，您需要按照以下步骤安装和运行该软件。这是一个好主意，拔掉您的加密狗，然后再继续。
1.下载并安装虚拟音频线。我们使用SoundFlower for Mac(@sophed)和VB Cable for Windows(@sashae)。
1.下载安装SDR#。由于SDR#涉及需要更换驱动程序的批安装程序，请参阅本SDR#快速入门指南。
4.设置音频采样率。在左面板中，向下滚动以查看音频设置。选择采样&gt；192,000样本/秒。采样率取决于您电脑的声卡，因此可能是192,000个采样/秒不可用，在这种情况下，请尝试选择较低的采样率，如48,000个采样/秒。
5.调整音频输入和输出设置。确保音频输入是您计算机的声卡。对于@sashae，这意味着从输入列表中选择Mme Microsoft声卡。确保音频输出是您的虚拟音频电缆。
8.调谐到一个频率：在频率显示框中，点击每个数字的上半部分可以增加频率，点击下半部分可以减少频率。您还可以使用瀑布显示单击、拖动和滚动频率。
9.更改模式：点击左侧面板上标有AM、NFM、WFM、USB、LSB、CW的气泡，即可更改软件解调数据的模式。要接收来自NOAA卫星的信号，请选择无线调频或宽调频。
10.调整带宽：在左侧面板中，找到带宽窗口；使用您的数字键或向上/向下小箭头来增加或减少带宽。要接收卫星传输，请选择36,000-45,000 kHz之间的带宽。
提示：您也可以使用鼠标：在瀑布显示中，单击频率范围的边缘，然后向左或向右拖动以手动增加或减少带宽。
提示：SDR#中的其他设置可以保持默认模式；例如，SDR#中的过滤器可以保持Blackman-Harris 4。
提示：您将被定向到GitHub上的最新版本的Cubi SDR。如果您看不到名称中含有Mac&34；的文件，请查找&#34；Darwin&34；。为什么？MacOS软件运行的是一款名为达尔文的开源软件。
3.在对话框窗口中，从Devices(设备)列表中选择您的加密狗，然后单击Start(启动)。例如，对于RTL-SDR加密狗，请选择通用RTL2832U OEM：：00000001&#34；。
提示：如果您还不知道，请找出您的加密狗的采样率。选择加密狗后，其采样率将显示在对话窗口右侧的表格中。把它记下来。
4.单击采样率，然后为您的加密狗选择正确的采样率。RTL-SDR加密狗的采样率为1.92 MHz。
提示：Cubi SDR在窗口底部栏中提供了有用的**悬停提示**以及有关命令和快捷键的信息。默认情况下，悬停提示处于打开状态，是了解软件的好方法。如果要将其关闭，请单击“设置”并取消选择“显示悬停提示”。花点时间探索一下吧！
6.调到一个频率。有三种简单的方法可以调到Cubi SDR中的频率。
在“中心频率”标签下，单击数字的上半部分和下半部分。
开始键入数字。将出现一个弹出窗口。完成所需频率的键入，然后在键盘上按Return键。请记住，默认的截止频率为兆赫，始终写为MHz。兆赫是等于一百万赫兹的频率单位。
7.要更改调制类型或模式，请在左上角选择AM、FMS、NBFM、AM、LSB、USB、DSB、I/Q。NOAA卫星传输为FM，这是调频的缩写。
8.调整带宽。在标签带宽下，单击数字的上半部分和下半部分。
9.调整音频输出音量。在左上角，单击并拖动垂直绿色条。
10.选择音频输出：单击左上角的音频输出下拉菜单，然后选择您先前设置的虚拟音频设备。在@sophed的案例中，这是SoundFlowers(2ch)&34；。
Cubi SDR在退出时可能会冻结。发生这种情况时，右键单击Cubi SDR图标并强制退出。
当您第一次启动WXtoImg时，系统将执行一系列步骤来为您的计算机量身定制该软件。通过这些步骤。然后按照下面的说明操作。
2.分配地面站位置。选择选项&gt；地面站位置(您可以搜索城市或手动输入您的纬度和经度)
3.更新开启器。选择文件&gt；更新Keplers(最好在每次卫星解码会话之前更新Keplers以保持准确性)(计算机必须连接到Internet才能正常工作)。
在出现的对话框中，选择您选择的虚拟音频电缆。选择声卡&gt；[您的虚拟音频电缆]。
输入接收卫星信号的仰角设置；提示：开始时设置良好：最大仰角在9度以上；仅当卫星在8度以上时才记录。
从天线类型列表中选择要使用的天线类型。有关@sashae使用的录制设置，请参见下图：
6.检查卫星通行证列表：选择文件&>卫星通行证列表。这将生成一个窗口，其中包含NOAA卫星将经过您的地面站位置的即将到来的时间)。
1.使用首选的SDR软件调谐到调频广播电台(约100 Mhz)。
提示：由于您使用虚拟音频电缆将来自SDR软件的音频传输到WXtoImg，您应该无法通过计算机扬声器听到电台的声音。如果您可以听到电台的声音，请调整您的音频输出。
3.WXtoImg窗口中是否开始加载充满小点或斑点的线条？如果是这样的话，WXtoImg可以听到您的无线电软件，并且音频连接正常。
4.右下角的音量是绿色的吗？如果它是绿色的，这意味着信号在适合解码的范围内。如果不是绿色(例如，如果是红色或黄色)，请调整SDR软件上的音量，直到WXtoImg音量变为绿色。
好的通行证是超过10分钟的通行证。在卫星通行证列表中，持续时间以分钟为单位。在WXtoImg生成的卫星通行证列表中，找到下一个11分钟以上的通行证。
卫星的运行方向是什么？在卫星通行证列表中，注意卫星是北向还是南向。还要注意卫星是在你的东边还是西边，以及最大海拔高度是多少。例如：NOAA 15在49W-(最大海拔)处的南下通道意味着卫星正在向南飞行，在其最大海拔(穿过通道的中间)，它将在90度(90度直接在头顶上方)中向西49度。这意味着，如果您使用的是Turnstile天线，则在通过过程的中途，您应该将天线对角向上对准西面，角度为49度。如果您使用的是V形偶极天线，如RTL-SDR v3中的V形偶极天线套件，则应使天线与地面平行，并保持南北方向(两个天线相距120度，均面向南)。
卫星发射的频率是多少？请务必记下您选择的卫星通行证的频率(请参阅卫星通行证列表中最右边的一列，其中频率以Mhz为单位列出)。
在你们当地的什么地方能最好地接收到这个信号？传输最好从地平线良好的开阔区域接收。这可以是公园、停车场、屋顶、小山、海滩或阳台(只要阳台可以清楚地看到卫星的路径)。
您是否使用36,000-45,000 kHz带宽，并使用FM(立方SDR)或宽带FM(SDR#)进行解调。
您的SDR软件是否正在向WXtoImg发送音频(请参阅上面的步骤以测试它们是否可以相互听到)？
在SDR#中，瀑布显示可能会冻结或卡顿。如果发生这种情况，只需关闭程序并重新打开它，保持您的加密狗处于连接状态。
1.距离通行证开始还有几分钟。在WXtoImg选择文件&>记录&>自动记录；当卫星经过您在记录选项对话框中指定的高程时，您应该开始接收信号。
2.卫星信号看起来像一系列微弱的点和虚线，将开始出现在您的SDR软件的瀑布显示屏上；
3.当您看到卫星信号时，调整频率(如果需要)，使SDR/无线电软件以信号为中心。这可能意味着增加1或2 mhz的频率。
4.调整多普勒频移。发射频率从略高于卫星指定频率漂移到略低于指定频率。在通过过程中，通过使用箭头或在瀑布显示中拖动来调整此多普勒频移，以使无线电软件以卫星信号为中心。
5.如果你使用的是Turnstile天线，记住当卫星在头顶绕轨道运行时要跟踪它。你可以让信号的看得见的力量指引你。如果你使用的是另一种天线，比如V形偶极子，你需要注意方向：保持天线与地面平行，南北方向(两只耳朵相距120度，都是朝南的)。
如果您将天线指向正确的方向与错误的方向，您将在瀑布显示屏上看到信号强度的显著差异。
6.定期检查WXtoImg，确认音量为绿色，PASS正在解码。
7.遍历结束后，WXtoImg将开始自动处理原始数据。你会看到软件正在工作，因为它结合了来自不同传感器的信息，产生了一系列黑白和彩色图像。完成后，窗口左下角将显示“处理完毕”。(#39；Done&#39；Processing&#39；Done&#39；Processing)
9.您刚刚收到的图像将存储在原始图像选项卡中。您可以双击刀路的原始图像，然后使用增强菜单查看传感器数据的不同组合。
10.此通行证特有的原始图像和音频文件也将保存在您的计算机上的WXtoImg&#39；S&39；Raw&39；和&#39；Audio&39；文件夹中。
我的卫星解码软件在测试过程中加载了一张黑色图像，这意味着您的SDR/无线电软件与WXtoImg之间的连接有问题。您需要仔细检查所有连接和虚拟音频电缆。仔细检查SDR软件上的音频输出和WXtoImg中的声卡(音频输入)。
音量处于红色，在测试期间接近0。这意味着进入WXtoImg的信号太安静，WXtoImg无法处理它。调高SDR软件的音量。如果这不起作用，则可能是将SDR软件连接到WXtoImg的音频输入和输出有问题。
@Sophed和@Sashae在试图学习如何解码气象卫星图像的过程中发现了许多其他有用的指南。他们发现，阅读不同的指南通常会澄清不同的问题，因为作者在这个过程中强调了不同的细节。这些指南包括：**。
如果您有其他问题、问题或顾虑，请在#露天项目页面上提问，或与@sophed和@sashae联系。
太棒了。关于如何入门的非常清晰、详细的解释。几年前我建造了。

by  sashae with   sophied |   June 26, 2020 17:12    26 Jun 17:12   ...     |      #24043

    sashae was awarded  the Empiricism Barnstar by  todgobblins for their work in this research note.
     This guide is a  Beta version and is currently being tested! Testing may take longer than planned as due to supply chain issues caused by the Covid-19 pandemic some hardware components might be temporarily unavailable. If you use the guide, please send  @sashae and  @sophied feedback: what worked, what was missing or could have been clearer?
 Guide for the reception of NOAA satellite images using software defined radio on Windows or MacOS
 We document here only a few ways to receive an Automatic Picture Transmission (APT) from active National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) satellites. Other software and hardware setups are possible! Importantly, the guide is limited to Windows and MacOS. Please get in touch if you would like to contribute instructions for Linux or another operating system.
     An  antenna capable of receiving circularly polarised transmissions: Turnstile (£38) We both use a turnstile antenna from AMSAT UK. AMSAT UK turnstile antennas come with the adapters and cables you need for satellite reception but no dongle---you need to purchase one separately.
 One or two  cables. We recommend two options: an RF cable for connection between antenna and dongle (£5--10). The RF cable should have 50 ohms impedance. Two metres is a good length. The cable needs a male SMA plug to fit into the dongle.
 a USB A male to USB A female extension cable (£5--10)  and a short cable or adapter to connect your dongle to your antenna. Ideally, the USB cable should be no longer than 3 metres to avoid interference. This set-up is based on the  RTL-SDR dongle kits (£35), which come with a dongle, adapter, extension cable as well as a portable dipole antenna.
 *Not all dongles are compatible with SDR# and CubicSDR. Before purchasing, check the dongle is compatible with the software you intend to use!
   Satellite decoding software For both Windows and Mac OS we use the beta version of  WXtoImg (free)
 MacOS 10.15 Catalina compatibility issues If you are running MacOS 10.15 Catalina you will not be able to download, install and use WXtoImg. This is because 10.15 Catalina no longer supports 32 bit applications. If you are in this position you have several options: Partition your Mac to run Windows 10 via Bootcamp. Then install your SDR software, virtual audio cable and WXtoImg on your Windows partition. You will need to enter the partition whenever you want to use the aforementioned software to decode NOAA APT transmissions.
 Use an alternative satellite decoding software that runs on Max OS Catalina, like  NOAA APT 1.2.0, an open source programme developed by Martin Bernardi. NOAA APT 1.2.0 has fewer features than WXtoImg, for example it does not have false-color images and live decoding. Instead of piping audio directly from SDR software to the satellite decoding software, as you can do with WXtoImg, in NOAA APT 1.2.0 will need to make a recording of the satellite transmission using your SDR software then you upload the WAV file.,The software produces reasonably large, high contrast and detailed images. See the activity post:  How to Compile NOAA APT 1.2.0 on Mac
 Use a browser-based APT Decoder, like  APT 3000 on Mac. Like NOAA APT 1.2.0, you will need to upload pre-recorded WAV files. The browser then generates the satellite images. The quality of images produced by APT 3000 is less than NOAA APT 1.2.0 and WXtoImg, but it is a simple alternative that does not require significant effort or time.
 Use a virtual machine like  Parallels to run Windows 10 on your Mac. This is a paid service that enables you to run Windows on your Mac without partitioning your hard drive. At the time of publishing a desktop license cost £69.99\.
  Once you've assembled the hardware, a day to half a day, a little patience and, ideally, a buddy to do things like hold your antenna while you tune the software.
     How do NOAA satellites collect data? The images transmitted by NOAA satellites are produced by the satellite's primary scanning instrument called the Advanced Very High Resolution Radiometer (AVHRR). The instrument is designed to detect five channels of radiant energy from the surface of the Earth ranging from the visible spectrum to the near-infrared and infrared or thermal spectra. As the satellite passes over a given part of the earth, the AVHRR sensors collect and transmit data in near-real time. Think of the satellite as scanning Earth's surface line by line. In the resulting images each pixel is approximately 4 × 4 km.
 How do they transmit data? The NOAA satellites have inbuilt radio antennas that transmit the data collected by the AVHRR instrument on a frequency in the 137 MHz range. To minimise interference between satellites, each NOAA satellite transmits on a different frequency within the 137 MHz range.
 How do NOAA satellites orbit? NOAA satellites orbit the Earth sun-synchronously. A sun-synchronous orbit is a nearly polar orbit in which the satellite passes over a given point of the planet's surface at the same time everyday. The NOAA satellites follow a Low Earth Orbit (LEO). A LEO is an Earth-centered orbit with an altitude below 2,000 km. Each satellite circles the Earth approximately every 100 minutes.
  What does the antenna do? Your antenna is a sensor. It catches electromagnetic waves and transforms them into an electrical current i.e. an electrical signal. All antennas are tuned to specific frequency ranges meaning that they receive or transmit these frequencies best. Most antennas are directional.
 What does the RF cable do? An RF cable is an insulated radio frequency cable. It includes a central conductor surrounded by a shield conductor. The cable carries electrical signals from one point to another (i.e. from your antenna to your dongle) without introducing interference.
 What does the dongle do? The dongle receives the electrical signal from the coaxial cable, filters it, and converts it from analogue to a digital I/Q so that it can be read by your computer.
  Why do you need software? You need software to process the digital signal sent to your computer by your dongle. Software makes these signals visible and audible; other software decodes the signals transmitted by the satellites into visual images.
 What are the different kinds of software needed? To decode transmissions from satellites, you need three kinds of software:
 Software Defined Radio or  SDR software to demodulate the radio waves caught by your antenna and tune to specific frequencies, like a traditional radio. SDR software outputs audio
 Satellite decoding software (e.g.  WXtoImg) to take the audio generated by the SDR software and decode it into an image
 A  virtual audio cable software to link the SDR software and the satellite decoding software...
 How can I practice using SDR software? The best way to practice using SDR technologies is to take your equipment (antenna, computer, dongle and cables) out into a nearby open space, plug everything in, launch your SDR software, and have fun exploring the radio spectrum. You can try things like changing modes (modulation), scrolling through ham radio bands, and switching frequencies. See below for start up guide to SDR software.
  How do you connect your SDR software to your satellite decoding software? No matter what kind of computer you have and what kind of SDR software you prefer, you need to send the audio output from your SDR software to your satellite decoding software (e.g. WXtoImg). You can use a range of free software to act as the 'virtual cable' between the SDR software and satellite decoding software.  @sophied uses Soundflower (for Mac) while  @sashae prefers VB Cable (for Windows 10).
     1. Connect your antenna to your  RF cable. (If you need to assemble your antenna do this first)
 2. Screw the coaxial cable into the non-USB side of the dongle (Be careful not to over tighten the cable connector as they are easily destroyed. No more than finger tight)
  This is the basic set up that you will use to decode weather satellite images. In order for this set-up to become operational, you need to follow the steps below to install and run the software. It's a good idea to unplug your dongle before you continue.
  1. Download and install a virtual audio cable. We use  Soundflower for Mac ( @sophied) and  VB Cable for Windows ( @sashae).
       1. Download and install  SDR#. Since SDR# involves a batch installer that requires an exchange of drivers, please refer to this  SDR# Quick Start Guide.
     4. Set the Audio  Samplerate. In the left panel, scroll down to view the Audio settings. Select  Samplerate >  192,000 sample/second. Sample Rate is dependent on your PC's soundcard, so it may be that 192,000 sample/sec is not available, in which case try selecting a lower sample rate, like 48,000 sample/sec.
 5. Adjust  Audio Input and  Output settings. Make sure the audio input is your computer's soundcard. For  @sashae this means selecting  MME Microsoft soundcard from the input list. Make sure the audio output is your virtual audio cable.
     8. Tune to a  frequency: In the frequency display box, click on the upper half of each number to increase, and the lower half to decrease frequency. You can also click, drag and scroll through frequencies using the waterfall display.
   9. Changing  Modes: click bubbles labelled  AM, NFM, WFM, USB, LSB, CW at the left panel to change the mode in which data is demodulated by the software. For receiving transmissions from NOAA satellites, Choose  WFM or "Wide FM".
   10. Adjusting  Bandwidth: in the left panel, find the window for bandwidth; use your number keys or the small up / down arrows to increase or decrease bandwidth. For receiving satellite transmissions, select a bandwidth between  36,000 - 45,000 KHz.
 Tip: You can also use your mouse: in the waterfall display, click on the edge of the frequency range and drag left or right to manually increase or decrease bandwidth.
  Tip: Other settings in SDR# can remain in default modes; For example the Filter in SDR# can remain  Blackman-Harris 4.
     Tip: You will be directed to the most recent version of CubicSDR on GitHub. If you cannot see a file with "Mac" in its name, look for "Darwin". Why? MacOS software runs off a piece of open source software called Darwin.
  3. In the dialog window select your dongle from the  Devices list and click  Start. For example, for the RTL-SDR dongle select "Generic RTL2832U OEM :: 00000001".
   Tip: If you do not know it already, find out the sample rate of your dongle. When the dongle is selected, its sample rate will be displayed in the table on the right side of the dialogue window. Make a note of it.
 4. Click  Sample Rate and select the correct sample rate for your dongle. The sample rate for the RTL-SDR dongle is 1.92 MHz.
       Tip: CubicSDR offers useful **Hover Tips** and information on commands and shortcuts in the bottom bar of the window. Hover Tips are switched on by default and are a great way to get to know the software. If you want to turn them off, click  Settings and deselect  Show hover tips. Take time to explore these!
 6. Tune to a  frequency. There are three easy ways to tune to a frequency in CubicSDR
 Under the label 'Centre Frequency', click on the upper and lower parts of the numbers.
   Begin typing a number. A pop-up window will appear. Complete typing the frequency you want and, on your keyboard, press Return. Remember, the default until is Megahertz, always written as MHz. Megahertz is a unit of frequency equal to one million hertz.
   7. To change "Modulation type" or  modes, on the top left select  AM, FMS, NBFM, AM, LSB, USB, DSB, I/Q. NOAA satellite transmissions are  FM, which is the acronym for Frequency Modulation.
 8. Adjust the bandwidth. Under the label  Bandwidth, click on the upper and lower parts of the numbers.
 9. Adjust the  audio output volume. In the top left, click and drag the vertical green bar.
 10. Select the  audio output: click the audio-out dropdown in the top left corner and choose your virtual audio device that you previously setup. In  @sophied 's case this is " Soundflower (2ch)".
 CubicSDR can freeze when quitting. When this happens, right click on the CubicSDR icon and Force Quit.
   When you first launch WXtoImg, you will be taken through a series of steps to tailor the software to your computer. Go through the steps. Then follow the instructions below.
 2. Assigning your  Ground Station Location. Select Options > Ground Station Location (you can either search for a city or input your latitude and longitude manually)
   3. Update  Keplers. Select  File >  Update Keplers (It is good to update Keplers before every satellite decoding session to maintain accuracy) (the computer must be connected to the Internet for this to work)
    In the dialogue box that appears, select your chosen virtual audio cable. Select Soundcard > [Your Virtual Audio Cable]
  Input elevation settings for receiving satellite signals; Tip: good settings to begin: maximum elevation above  9 degrees; record only when satellite is above  8 degrees.
 Select what kind of antenna you are using from the Antenna Type list. See image below for recording settings employed by  @sashae:
   6. Checking the  Satellite Pass List: Select  File >  Satellite Pass List. This will generate a window with upcoming times at which NOAA satellites will pass over your ground station location)
   1. Tune to an FM radio station (around 100 Mhz) using your preferred SDR software
 Tip: Since you are piping the audio coming from the SDR software to WXtoImg with a virtual audio cable you should not be able to hear the station through your computer speakers. If you can hear the station, adjust your Audio Output.
  3. Do lines filled with tiny dots or speckles start loading in the WXtoImg window? If so, WXtoImg can hear your radio software and the audio connection is working.
  4. In the bottom right, is the volume level green? If it is 'in the green' this means the signal is in a good range for decoding. If it is not green (e.g. if it is red or yellow) adjust the volume on your SDR software until the WXtoImg volume becomes green.
    A 'good' pass is a pass that is more than 10 minutes long. In the Satellite Pass list, Duration is measured in minutes. In the Satellite Pass list generated by WXtoImg, find the next 11+ minute pass.
    What direction is the satellite traveling? In the Satellite Pass List, note whether the satellite is Northbound or Southbound. Also note whether the satellite will be to the east or west of you, and at what maximum elevation. For example: a NOAA 15 Southbound pass at 49W MEL (Maximum Elevation) means the satellite is traveling south and at its max elevation (mid way through the pass) it will be 49 degrees out of 90 (where 90 is directly overhead) to the west. This means that mid way through the pass, if you are using a Turnstile antenna, you should be aiming your antenna diagonally upward to the west at a 49 degree angle. If you are using a V-Dipole antenna like that in the "RTL-SDR v3 with the V-dipole antenna kit" you should keep the antenna parallel to the ground with a north-south orientation (and the two antenna 'ears' 120 degrees apart both facing South).
 What frequency will the satellite be transmitting on? Make sure to note down the frequency of your chosen satellite pass (see the rightmost column in the Satellite Pass List where Frequency is listed in Mhz).
 Where can you best receive this transmission in your local area? The transmission is best received from a wide open area with a good horizon. This could be a park, a parking lot, a rooftop, a hill, a beach or a balcony (as long as the balcony has a clear view of the path of the satellite)
  Are you using a 36,000--45,000 KHz bandwidth and demodulating with FM (CubicSDR) or Wide FM (SDR#)
 Is your SDR software sending audio to WXtoImg (see above steps for testing that they can hear each other)?
 In SDR# the waterfall display can freeze or stutter. If this happens simply close the programme and reopen it, keeping your dongle plugged in.
  1. A few minutes before the pass is due to start. On WXtoImg Select  File >  Record >  Auto Record; When the satellite passes over the elevation you designated in the Recording Options dialogue box, you should start receiving a signal.
 2. The satellite signal looks like a faint series of dots and dashes that will begin to appear on the waterfall display of your SDR software;
   3. When you see the satellite signal, adjust the frequency (if needed) so that the SDR / radio software is centred on the signal. This might mean increasing the frequency 1 or 2 MHz
 4. Adjust for the  Doppler shift. The frequency of the transmission 'drifts' from a little higher than the satellite's designated frequency to a little lower than the designated frequency. During the pass adjust for this Doppler shift by using the arrows or dragging in the waterfall display to keep the radio software centred on the satellite signal.
 5. If you are using a Turnstile antenna, remember to  track the satellite as it orbits overhead. You can let the visible strength of the signal guide you. If you are using another antenna like a V-Dipole you need to pay attention to orientation: keep the antenna parallel to the ground with a north-south orientation (and the two antenna 'ears' 120 degrees apart both facing South).
 You will see a marked difference in signal strength on the waterfall display if you are pointing the antenna in the right direction vs. the wrong direction.
 6. Periodically check WXtoImg to make sure the volume is green and the pass is being decoded.
   7. When the pass is over, WXtoImg will begin to automatically process the raw data. You will see the software 'working' as it combines information from different sensors to produce a series of b/w and colour images. When it is done processing 'Done' will appear in the bottom left of the window.
  9. The image you have just received will be stored in the  Raw Images tab. You can double click on the raw image of the pass and then use the Enhancements menu to look through the different combinations of sensor data.
 10. The raw image and audio file unique to this pass will also be saved in WXtoImg's 'Raw' and 'Audio' folders on your computer.
     My satellite decoding software loads a black image during the test This means there is something wrong in the connection between your SDR / radio software and WXtoImg. You need to double check all of the connections and virtual audio cables. Double check the Audio Output on your SDR software and the Soundcard (Audio Input) in WXtoImg.
 The volume is in the red, close to 0 during the test This means that the signal coming into WXtoImg is far too quiet for WXtoImg to be able to process it. Turn up the volume on your SDR software. If this doesn't work there may be an issue with the audio inputs and outputs connecting the SDR software to WXtoImg.
